[[File:Paradise gets that wrong.png|link=|center|750px]]



[[File:First picture.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


Dawn. The barn screamed as the cows burned. Nobody saw the fire. Grandmother woke to a brief glow, then blackness. Even the unlit menorah was gone by morning. Soot on the sill, fingerprints melted into the plastic. “I can’t,” Mother gasped. “I can’t believe this country.” Grandmother almost tripped. “David! Tea!” she yelled. David moved between them. The floor scratched beneath his feet. Old, burnt marks. “The— The barn,” he said. “What about it?” Grandmother’s cane slipped. The sound it made was the same one the barn made when it fell. “We need to see what’s left,” continued David. “We need to—” “For?” He paused. Almost slipped on the friction. Then David turned toward Mother, running toward the door. Grandmother shrieked. She ran to the door, before turning her head toward him briefly. “Call your Pa!” David paused. Legs shook. “But what about Ma?” “Just call him, David. Now.” The kitchen. His toe caught the plank, and he went down hard, shoulder into the pillar. The house shook. Splinters hid under his nails. Blood slicked the floor. The phone swung into view on its cord. He reached. Tried. Missed. Reached again. The buttons blurred. He pressed until something answered. Pa. “Pa?” “Hello. Wait. How’d you know my number?” David looked up to find his grandmother next, but the house was empty. The glass was still in the sink, tealess. “Come home, Pa.” An exhale. The sound filled the line. Shouts thinned into whispers. David glanced at the window—Grandmother chasing Mother. Her wrinkly skin clung to her leg bone. The sky sank under the wooden frame, leaf-pale, closing them. “Sigh. We’ve been through this for a while, haven’t we—” Breathing flickered. David swallowed, but the saltiness made it worse. “Grandma needs you home.” His lips trembled. “Bye.” Itchy in the eye. He rubbed his face as he stepped outside, and his fingers stuck. The sky was clear. Around them were orange trees dotting green ones, their trunks ashen black. And, in the middle, a frail woman pulled someone invisible from the hill, her limbs shaking. Rain came down, and it only hit his cheeks and knees. The itch on the cheeks and throat returned as Mother kneeled before the black mass. Grandmother stepped on the black floor barefoot and lifted the amalgamations with only her thumb. Now, the orange trees watched. Each leaf was an eye. Fall.










[[File:Second2 picture.png|link=|center|505px]]


Father stood in front of the screen before shifting to the side. Limbs tense. Breathing heavy. “Honey”, he finally uttered. “Food’s fine. Soup kitchens are free.” The television showed rubble. AFGHANISTAN. “Just the streets that aren’t safe,” Mother said. “Medicine isn’t free.” Wringing the towel, droplets falling between the floorboards, David rubbed her forehead while kneeling. Grandmother lay on a blanket on the kitchen floor. Eyes closed by wrinkled, thin skin. You could not tell if she was dead without listening. “I work a good job,” Father said. Tick. One o’clock. Moonlight caught the table’s corner. Father stood there, shoes still laced. Mother’s legs were hidden behind the table. “Look,” he continued. “We’ll figure this out tomorrow. Okay, Honey?” David heeded the white noise. Stomach growls hidden beneath occasional cricket chirps. He stood up and glanced at the bowl of honey on a larger plate, where apple peels and seeds were scattered. A knife hung over the table’s round edge. Mother was looking down. Moonlight passed through the foliage and landed on the blade, shining brighter than a lamp, just for a second. And she glanced up. The knife beside her. She gulped and tried reaching for the tablecloth, but she froze every time she moved a centimeter. Father sighed again and faced away from them. “Not my fault. Not my fault,” he whispered. Mother gazed up. Exhaled with her mouth. “We’ve got no dinner… You didn’t bring shit.” She stepped closer. “Come on,” Father said. She came to the table and dragged her hand across it. Closer. “I said we have no dinner!” The other hand lashed out. It almost hit him. Her fingers stopped inches from the handle. David blinked. Painful—no longer itchy. He jolted. Feet flung forward. Toes hit the table, nails cut deep into the sensitive skin. But his eyes were more painful. “I’ll do it, Ma,” David stepped in. “I’ll walk the I-95 to buy food.” The sore hit him. He bent down to press his toe both ways, distracting him from the pulsating ache. As he pushed down further, ignoring the splinters, a red spot emerged from the surrounding pale, yellow skin. Was it blood? Blood vessels trying to climb out of his skin? David gasped for air. The shadows moved in rhythm with the footsteps. Silent. Crickets talked. Grandmother on the floor, no longer staring at the window. Mother, however, remained while Father retreated to the grown-ups’ bedroom, and she reached for a chair that screeched on the floor, shaking the house, but paused to look at Grandmother. She lifted the furniture with both hands and placed it down slowly. “Sit,” Mother said, head tilted at David’s toe. He complied. The knife remained on the table. Mother grabbed it. Walking toward Grandmother, she stopped and squatted down to open a cabinet. Lifting the blade to her mouth, she bit it with her teeth. Her other hand searched, while the other grabbed the towel to feel the warm forehead. She kept her eyes on Grandmother. The eyes must itch. “David, please make me tea. Cream and sugar, too.” He compressed his lips. Bit his tongue to distract himself. “It pains down there.” She took out a white box from the darkness with a red cross in the center. Prying it open, some discarded pills spilled onto the floor. It was the roller bandages she snatched. The godly object. He almost slipped into a daydream, but only a shadow remained in the menorah decoration’s place. Mother grabbed the knife from her teeth. The dimness made the white cylinder a light source, at least visible like the clock. “Do you know what Advil is, David?” Mother said, still staring at Grandmother. Before he could respond, she turned back at him. She wrapped her hand with the long bandage as she put the white box on the table, pointing the knife upward so nobody dared to touch it. “You mean who?” David asked. The pain. He looked up. Nothing but more wooden planks. Hissing. “Hurry up, Ma. Please,” he murmured. “Can you think, David?” Mother questioned. David heaved his leg. It stung to keep it dangling, as the chair didn’t have bars to put his feet on. She dragged the blade across the bandage, but when her hands approached his wound, she paused mid-air. “Can you walk the I-95 alone?” He nodded, neck moving independently. But the real torment was on his knees. Made him realize his movement. “This isn’t the real world yet, David.” He ground his teeth from side to side. A broken jaw would be a distraction, too, he thought. “Why’re you this hard on me?” Mother stopped. She leaned forward and wrapped his toe with the bandage, gluing it with brown tape. “It still hurts,” he grunted. “You’re about to go to college, David. Pa told me you need to learn.” “Pa? Grandma would never do this, Ma.” As Mother tore the white wrapping, she paused, still holding it like tissue she’d prepared for. “Can you tell me what Advil is?” “I don’t know!” Mother sighed. Her eyes must hurt again. “You walk the I-95 while you repeat that word, okay?” David exhaled. He looked at his toe. It was bright, standing out against the dirty, brown planks. “In one year, you’re going to college. It’s a big and scary world out there, David.”







[[File:Third picture.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


David crossed his legs and pressed his ankle against his toes. The leather was stiff and dirty, while the pain came back sharper than before. He leaned his elbow against a metal pole to stay upright. Stinging cold. But he needed to, and he waited seconds too long. A green sign hung above him, small and tired. WELCOME TO NICETOWN. No longer was his palm uncomfortably numb, as the money in his pockets bulged in the supposed isolation. Every corner and every dust was a predator waiting to jump. He passed an empty parking lot while cars rushed by beside him, too fast to hear. Cars rushed somewhere beyond the lots, too fast to place. On the horizon, nothing but two dotted lines of light. I-95, he told himself. Or Roosevelt Boulevard. It didn’t matter. No chance to say Advil. The sky was black and starless and a piston. It pressed down. Even twilight couldn’t fight it. The skyline had slipped behind the trees, and the streetlights smeared into the dark, like someone had rubbed them with a thumb. Everything tall made shorter. Everything that should’ve loomed folded in on itself. His knees bent when he was walking, almost falling with each step. Stop. A restaurant waited ahead, two crosswalks away. The stripes looked thinner than usual. Above the red canopy was the deli’s short name. He stood at the entrance, breathing through his mouth. The menu behind the glass. HAM SANDWICHES. GRILLED CHEESE. Letters peeling away at their ends. Dairy and meat mixed. Sharp footstep scraped the concrete behind him. “Ay! What’cha doing?” A scream in the distance. David jolted, standing on his toes for a few milliseconds. He turned around. A hooded teen. “Oh, I’m sorry,” David muttered, tilting his head down. “Who you think you is blocking out the door like that, white boy?” The teen glanced back and waved his hand. “Jamie, c’mere. It’s one of yo kind.” A pause. Shoes on concrete. A mere shadow, before emerging behind the teen. He was no taller than David, a few years older. “Where you come from?” the figure said. David observed him, his head going up and down. A gold chain around his neck. He reached into his pocket as if he’d got one, but his fingers dropped to his shirt’s neckline, pulling it. “I’m… I’m sorry?” David approached him. Jamie backed off, lifting his clothes. Something dull caught the light, pressed too close to the waist. “Test me, cracka ass.” David pointed at the darkness that didn’t respond. “Guys, I’m from… I’m from there—” “You’re not from here, are you?” Jamie’s eyes landed on David’s elbow. Probably pale. “You’ll make this right. What’s your name, hm?” “It’s… David. All right, I get this, okay? I’m just getting food.” “What you got in your pockets, David?” David gulped. Hands stayed on his shirt, snapping the threads. The pauses between the conversation were interrupted by the sound, but they didn’t falter. “I’ve only got enough to buy dinner—” “Pull it out.” “G-d save me,” he breathed. His shoulders rose, while every organ tightened. David reached into his pockets, crushing the money in his palm to make it harder for the thieves. He pulled it out. Only half of it stuck out of his pocket. “That’s at least a hundred bucks in there…” the teen teased Jamie. Jamie stepped forward. “You were gonna risk your life over a couple of bucks?” The duo chuckled, though their half-closed eyes still watched David. But as his hands gradually reached out, a lone car passed by slowly. Its tinted windows were a perfect mirror. As it disappeared from David’s view instead of stopping, it revealed the I-95’s lights like a curtain. Advil. Grandmother. Don’t dare forget, he thought. The money felt heavier in his hands. “Them ain’t the pigs you lookin’ for, white boy. Give me that, now.” Buy Advil. He breathed. Calm yourself, he thought. Advil was a medicine. Advil was a medicine. He’d got to tell Mother that. And so his fists tightened. David crunched the ball of money. Feet scraped the concrete. Ran. Where’s he going? What’s Philadelphia? The once-thin lines stretched closer to David. His feet landed on an edge, piercing through the leather and his skin. Behind him, the footsteps multiplied. They crushed pebbles. The cars passed on quickly without looking at him. The vehicles made the same cracking sound as they zoomed. David glanced behind him, but they didn’t stare back. Only the distant I-95. Hope it is. It was the I-95 and nothing else. What was it to him?. His legs veered him closer toward the parking lot, even as his shoes rubbed against the concrete platform—a reminder of the sidewalk’s edge. Yet, there was no bigger push, and the wind that blew at him made him push against it harder. He jumped over the bushes and landed on a streak of paint. Some branches stuck to his already throbbing ankle, scars on thin skin, but a few seconds spent on yanking them out meant a deeper slash. He kept running, and the wind tried to shoo him away, but he continued until a flickering light blinded him in the darkness. Not there before. A truck-sized entrance with a yellow sign hanging MAX HEIGHT OF 8 FEET. Underneath it was a slope to an underground parking lot. “Get him…” The thieves drawled too long. A pitch too low. David closed his eyes. Hear, O Israel. But he could not finish the prayer. There was only the word Advil. As he crossed the white arrow that pointed toward him, his feet landed on a grate. Ibuprofen. No clacking metals. Silence. His one leg slipped through the crack, followed by his entire body. (RS)-2-(4-
ㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤ(2-methylpropyl)
ㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤphenyl)
ㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤpropanoic
ㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤㅤacid.







[[File:fifth image.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


A plastic packet of brown powder lay on the floor. The hooded teen from earlier closed the door behind David as he sat on the staircase’s railings. “Don’t drop nun’, Rebbe.” “And don’t call me with that shit, Ty.” David bent down slightly to pick something up, but his legs didn’t touch the floor. “I’m gonna smack you—” “A’ight, chill, brother.” Tyrone squatted and took the plastic. “So, what we’re doing?” “I’ma tell you this, Bugsy,” Tyrone said, sighing. “If you gon’ sell, you gotta understand your customers, too. You know, morality-wise.” David looked down at the never-ending stair opening. Darkness on the downward horizon with a concealing fog as its friend. “I… I still go to the synagogue, Ty,” he said, glancing at the teenager. “I know my stuff. You already told me in the car.” “And I go to church. That’s why we’re friends, yo.” Tyrone dangled the plastic over David’s face as he made his way toward the staircase. “What’s this stuff?” “Fent—” Tyrone paused, sitting down. He coughed. “Actually, nah, nah. It’s dope on crack.” “That’s cool.” The light hit the packet and reflected on David’s face. Half-closing his eyes, he still managed to snatch it. Rubbing it, the texture was like a softer asphalt. Tiny stones crushed, though dirt-colored. His eyes and hands felt dusty and oily, but still handled the packet despite it being more disgusting. He opened it, and his nose joined in with the party. “Don’t sniff it, Bug.” “You mean… OD?” Tyrone nodded, his eyes accepting the fear lingering behind them. “I’m fine, Ty. Don’t worry about it.” David leaned down and inhaled. Subtle sound of snot. Every powder sucked into his nostrils exploded like fireworks. The floor thinned. The walls caved in. David’s hands. Half of his hands. He clung to the railing, but only his ring and pinky did. And he stared down at the horizon at the staircase. Tyrone’s voice deepened. A pitch too low. Underneath, the horizon stared at him when he reached up to scratch his jaw. A void. The fog now bore his face. The eyes gazed back at him. David jolted, almost falling on himself. He could not see beyond them—vision stolen.







[[File:Fourth image.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


Break. He awoke when oily dirt shot up his pupils, while his legs lay flat. Purple skin on his foot. Felt like a needle-stuffed pillow when he moved it across the floor. His ankles moved freely. Biting his lips again to turn the pain bearable, he dragged himself with his palms until he could lie on a pillar. Each time his muscle moved, the lungs paused, trachea blocked by pain. Eyes still hurt. Scratched it. More sting. He blinked, and sleep fell off his eye, but jagged enough to irritate his brows. A subtle, pleasant hum rang in his ear. Shin covered in black sludge. Eye aches subsided. From above, the fluorescent lights shone on the grime, reflecting it, making it seem solid. Pathetic bondage. He stood up on his left foot while his right faltered. Flickers. The sudden change made him glance around. An abandoned lobby? Arrays of pillars stretched as far as the I-95, but the sky was pitch-black. There was no visible highway to light the night. He took a step and stood on one foot. Even without a path to lead him like the interstate’s dim streetlamps or a functional ankle, he needed to go home. Yet, he’d not found any kosher restaurant nor Advil for Grandmother. His head tilted instinctively onto the tiles. “I’m so sorry,” he mouthed. Wait. Beside him, a door leading to a stairwell. Oh, G-d. He reached into his nose while grabbing his wallet from his pocket. The brown sugar. The plastic packet. His fingers quivered, goosebumps all over his body. Seconds turned into hours. No. No blood. No powder. Nothing minty. When he tried sighing in relief, the barbed pain at his foot shut his mouth, and he grunted instead. He kept walking and rubbed his fingers over the bills, the cleanest-looking dirty thing he could see. Abraham Lincoln, his only friend. A comfortable face to whisper while walking through this building. Any face would do. Behind him was nothing, and Lincoln faced left at the staircase leading down from David. Was he always facing left? But the pain kept him walking. He crouched down and, dragging himself, limped with each step. The thumps added, and the pain multiplied. Sore at the bottom of his spine. David grabbed the Lincoln bill tightly to walk better until it became the shape of a bow. In the distance, the repeating pillars made them appear to stack and form a singular wall. Bare concrete as the floor. The staircase he descended stretched across his vision. It didn’t speak loudly enough. His pain propelled him forward into the darkness, as the “wall” disintegrated into a pattern of interchanging black-and-white bars. If only he could grasp them, and they would pull him forward. Everything passes, but nothing moves. By the end, his eyebrows returned to touching the greasy, sandy concrete, and he lay on the white arrow in a fetal position. Above him, the lights warmed. Beside him, the walls thinned into aluminum panels. The floor, ragged. He reached into his pockets and grabbed the wallet again. Hands lifted, resting his elbow on the ragged floor. The wallet hovered over the light, as the dust around its edges swayed like hair. David opened it. A crumpled bill fell out and hit the side of his eye. The Lincoln bill. Abraham still faced left, but his face became grotesque. Fragmented. Torn. The paper betrayed David and his only friend. He needed to speak, but his foot still disallowed. David looked up while he reached down to massage the bruised ankle. A sign written in Japanese. Maybe there was a pattern. Maybe each stroke on those characters meant an English word. Maybe, even if he was wrong, the meaning still overlapped. But he collapsed again from the pain and almost hit his head on the concrete. Grunts. The other side of the wall. His eyes felt heavy to move. While lying, he stepped on his left foot and dragged himself, rotating so his head could see whether the wall ended. Colorful lights. He stood up using his knee and crept over. Whispering to himself, looking down at the dirty floor, he closed his eyes once more. Blessed are you, O L-rd, who revives the dead. Grunts. David looked up.







[[File:Sixth image.png|link=|center|505px]]


From the side of the aluminum panels, confetti bright as the sun lay on a parking spot. The concrete intersected with those sharp triangles and squares, and they pierced through each other like they only existed in the eye. What are you? David thought. It is confetti. These forever-fleeting sparkles were alive—a single entity. He is confetti. He is just as real. David reached out to touch it. Got repelled. But when he blinked, the pain went away. It was a few years ago in a synagogue. The Bar Mitzvah confetti exploded. They flew for too long, and the air around David was merely confetti. He couldn’t breathe. The pain? Still none. Uncomfortable straps around his arm and head, pressing onto his skin until it was pale and numb. When it came loose at his elbow, he tried tightening it. He glanced up as his neck was sore. An eye in the darkness. Beside it, a Hanukkiah. It wasn’t yet December. The pupil was a perfect circle. The distant row of lights, the I-95. But the brightest, its white sclera, was polluted with veins. And they thickened. Each second a minute too long. Then it burst. Then the confetti exploded. When? How? He squeezed his eye. The agony in the foot returned. Thank G-d. David turned toward the light again. His fists tightened, legs faltering. He dropped to the ground. With veins popping out of his forehead, he clenched the floor as his fingernails pressed against a wide crack. Jolt. A concrete fragment. It flew into the light. Explosion. Then another. It kept dividing like a fractal. Some of the dust landed on his face. Although he rubbed it away, a ticklish sensation remained on his temple. David got himself up, shooing the floating debris away like a mosquito. He sat down, facing the light. His Campfire. “What are you, Campfire?” The wind blew from outside. Campfire interwove the zephyrs like fabric with its tendrils, grunting. Made the atoms visible. His light shone on David’s eyes, but it didn’t make anyone blink. “You look painful, you know that?” David glanced toward his purple ankle. “Do I look the same?” Silence. His green squares swam around the air and disappeared into the darkness, while the red flares shot down and carved a slight hole, peeling away the paint. “Sigh. You are what you are. The sugar packet, was that you, too?” A rush of cold cascaded around David, shutting him and his thoughts. He kept staring at Campfire. “You must be cold. Why can’t you touch anything?” Still, no response. David drooped down. His posture bent. Carefully placing his palm over his right foot, he curled into a fetal position. The bottom of his spine pressed onto the floor. Pulsated. But he swallowed it all down. He thought about the eye on the window, and that was enough. In the abyss beside him, a dot of warm light emerged. Was that the eye? He turned toward the light and asked him the same question. Nothing but configuring reality. It had endless power. But infinity, like zero, never fails to be dreadfully disappointing. Never did Campfire blink, nor did its light fail to freeze the air around it, except for a mild breeze. Regardless, it was dead. Campfire’s relentless light turned him away, and he fixated on the faraway eye. It was a period. A speck smaller than the concrete fragments, in a space that was boundless but still inferior to this scruffy room. What did it mean to be small? What did it mean to be big and powerful? The dot blinked, though only once. No way for Morse code, even if David could translate. The eye from years ago didn’t, however. It merely stared until he made his way toward his birthday cake. Father was on a call. The disembodied voices on his telephone were like Campfire’s, but they were screaming. Clinging to Father’s coat, David asked who it was. Father didn’t respond. He whispered something that was drowned by the yelling, and he walked out the door into the darkness. David chased, but he tripped and landed face-first on the grass. The bayit on his head dug deep into his skin, leaving a straight scar, where its ends poked deeper from the box’s sharp corners. When he returned toward Mother for a bandage, he looked out the window. The eye disappeared. David never got a bandage, he remembered. Back on the concrete floor, he stared at the bland void of the night. The speck disappeared, but Campfire remained. David stood up, limping, but still managed to turn around and land a spit. “You are the cold itself,” he said. “I trusted you, Campfire.” His saliva crisscrossed the shaking, sharp shapes of Campfire, and it swirled around to make a circular mark.







[[File:Eighth image.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


Every four steps with one foot, he hugged the pillars to keep himself standing. A good exercise, physical and mental. He could breathe and look around without scratching or wiping his face, even if his foot felt worse. The building was pleasing to observe. Bare, almost skeletal, as pillars and unpainted blotches on the roof, easy for the mind to fixate on. They were not alive, unlike Campfire. He could whisper to them, and they couldn’t judge, he thought. But enough of Campfire. Backtracking to emotions made his legs tighten, the blood rushed in torn veins, flooding his foot. He felt his chest close in on itself. And then his stomach spoke again, rumbling. He felt his tongue, cottony and thirsty. It was a beautiful feeling. A simple reminder that scattered his thoughts. Who painted these walls? Who built these pillars? Who let everything crumble? The peeling paint smelled oddly of spoiled meat and eggs. But the charred texture of those walls cut through the rancid aroma. He could only think of the smoked ham. The beef, no? The floating non-kosher sign no longer existed. Those were to be ignored. Food and Advil. Just those. Grabbing his wallet, he took out all the money he had. A few pennies fell as it was dragged by the paper bills, but he still had enough. The twilight had not arrived. Dinner would be at least expectedly late. Searching around, a giant text spanned across a wall. LEVEL 594. No reflections on its black paint; its edges were straight without blotches. Total blackness. Truly something to admire. He took a few seconds before glancing away to the endless space again. Such a silly thought, he mouthed. And so he turned toward the black paint again. When he exhaled, he breathed out mist that flew to his side. Campfire’s coldness followed him. David stared at the paint lines. Was there not an infinite void behind the cavity? He could reach and touch the number, but his foot dropped. Yet, his hands could still tightly grab the thin, gray pillar. He felt a downward slope. “A change is a change,” he said, and so he followed it. He looked up, and the floor’s layout stayed the same. But the number was no longer 4. LEVEL 593. And it was the same, featureless black. What were letters and numbers but authoritarians for the mind? He swallowed. “I curse you,” he said. David blinked. He glanced to the side without the mist to guide him, where his eyes landed on a carved-out space with small windows. Half of them were ajar, held up by bars welded between their black frames and the shadowed walls. The same fog that stared at him in the stairwell. He blinked. This time, no eyes. He felt his pockets and retrieved the coins that’d fallen before, though more carefully this time, so he wouldn’t need to pick them up from the ground again. Placing them flat on his hands, he rubbed them for the last time. With his middle and ring fingers, he got it between the coins, clutched them, and flung them. Each time it hit the wall, it was merely a click, devoid of echoes. They stopped after the fourth and didn’t continue after, and the last one he could see happened as the coin disappeared into the darkness. Minutes passed with his eyes dilated. Ears focused hard enough, ringing, but there was no indication that the coin ever hit the ground.




[[File:Seventh image.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


He took out his wallet in search of another coin. Rummaging through strings and strips of paper, he jolted his head side-to-side to find a telephone. The red ones with a metal box. Emergencies only. As he struggled to run, he looked out into the empty void that wasn’t the night sky. There were no staring eyes. Not even a distant, flickering light. He told her to sit since she was old and had just recovered from a sickness, continuing, “It depends, Grandma.” “What were they about?” The thought of tea made him glance for a millisecond. Nothing. Not even a kettle. “Both… both were at night,” he said, “and I saw Ma. Anyways, the first one had me in sweat.” “You’ve got to get closer to G-d, then.” “I tried.” “Well, then, try harder.” “I did, Grandma. I did. I was running in my first dream. My heart kept pounding like it’d split open, and I couldn’t close it again. I really thought I’d die there in… in that forest. There was a cabin by some miracle. I hid inside and stayed quiet. I didn’t say, I didn’t move. I watched through the window. There was an animal out there, like a tiger. But then it looked at me in the window, and its eyes were wrong. Red, but not blood. Looked like a petal was covering it. That’s all, though. Just a bad dream. The second one had Mother in it. It was less bad.” “It ain’t good?” “Yeah. It wasn’t. But it was the happiest moment I’d had in days. She was driving. The car was probably rented. We were on a bridge. Wide. A dozen lanes, I think. A green sign said Manhattan. The radio was playing a New York song I didn’t know. She was singing along, loud. She kept her eyes on the road. I never thought she’d do that. The nearest car was far off. Just small lights through the window. Distant. Untouchable. She watched them anyway and kept driving slowly. Her hands were shaking on the wheel. Just a little. The rest of her was still. I think I spoke to her, then she turned her head strangely. The bridge kept going after that. Too long. The lights… didn’t end. They felt infinite. When I touched her shoulder, I saw it. Her neck wasn’t right. It was wrong, like the tiger was.” A sharp sting in his eyes. Felt purple in his ankles. Pulsations inside his eyebrows. It then became wet, and he wiped it with his unwashed hands. Stung more. Grandma looked at him. Her neck was old and marked by thousands of wrinkles. It moved naturally, unlike Mother. As she reached to pat him on his shoulder, it drifted toward his ear instead. “What happened to your ears? What’s with the holes, David?” “Holes?” he spoke suddenly. “It’s like you got a perfect circle punched right in. Hold on. I’m getting a mirror.” While she got up, slowly cracking her back, David whistled for her attention. “Wait, Grandma. You need to rest.” She chuckled. “I’ll get more tired telling you where it is.” David replied with a smile, but his head turned away. He rubbed his earlobes with two fingers, feeling the cleanly punched hole through its underside. “Can you get some stitches, too?” “Okay, honey.” He tilted his head out of instinct. Not sure. Grandma’s robes swung and disappeared behind a wall. The floorboards creaked and didn’t echo. In these few minutes, he slouched down on the table and rested his head on his arm like they were pillows. When periodic discomfort made him shift, he’d look up for a brief second. Kitchen counter. Not a window frame. The menorah had appeared by the second. Lit. Seven stalks and seven flames, he counted. One on both ends, missing. Even blinked multiple times, then half-closed his eyes and circled them like glasses to ensure it didn’t only exist in his eye. Still, seven instead of nine. Immediately as he prepared to stand up to investigate, Grandma arrived. “Here, I’ve got you some tea, too—” “Grandma. Why’ve you got a… menorah in here?” She tilted her head, furrowing one eyebrow. “What’d you mean?” “Hanukkah’s in a few months,” he hesitated to speak. The air was denser, despite the warm sunlight. David tried to humor her. “Did you convince the rabbi to give you things again?” And a mosquito landed on her arm, but it shrieked and flew away. The same one from earlier. It then settled on his. He smacked it. The black mass of insect legs and juices. A dark, red smudge. Flower. Look up. Neck ached. The same soreness he imagined Mother also had. The painting reappeared. Moved positions. A meter to the left. “What’s that?” Grandma asked. “Grandma…” She stared with dilated eyes. “What’s… what?” “Does it hurt?” “Uh. No?” “What does it feel like?” Without looking down at the mosquito’s crushed body, he wiped its juices away with the side of his palms. “Doesn’t feel like anything? It’s… It’s like a needle that’s too small for you to feel.” “Well. Let’s hope this needle is too. Right?” Grandma said. She smiled. David let out a nervous chuckle, fading out into a sigh as she picked up the needle from the table. Her eyes trembled under the weight of her wrinkles. She picked up the needle and thread with one hand, moving her chair closer to him. Slip. Her fingers let the needle fall. Thank G-d it landed perpendicularly to the cavities between the floorboards. Before she could reach down, David stopped her. He told her that she needed to rest instead. She complied. He grabbed the needle with ease and brought it up where she could simply take it. However, the sharp point was directed toward her. A tingling sensation on his arm, as if the mosquito was still there. In that split second, his eyes landed upon another. Merely a black dot. It flew to Grandma’s face, rubbed its snout, and tried sucking her blood out. Its wings retracted, and it fell to the floor like a speck of heavy dust. And in that moment, Grandma’s fingers touched its pointed end. Pop. Skin ruptured. David staggered. Stood up. Couldn’t feel his feet. A kaleidoscopic view. The kitchen, a swirl of colors. That knife box. The handle became bigger, And bigger, And bigger, He grabbed it. He looked toward the door. “Grandma? Grandma?” Brought a knife to a gunfight. Grandma merely got a needle. Go. He sprinted toward the door. Stubbed his toe. Felt numb. The painting now hung on the door, and the pain from his toe returned. Tangled sunflowers stared. Disappeared from view when he ran outside and swung the door closed.







[[File:Seventeenth image.png|link=|center|505px]]


He pushed the knife against his thigh, almost too hard. The blade ran through the fabric and barely reached the skin. Run more slowly? No. That meant death. Pennsylvania and the grassy knoll beside him were no more than parking lots and the abyss outside. He could lie down there, blind his eyes with the autumn sun, while waiting for the rest of the congregation to find Grandma terrified, hands covering her mouth in a wardrobe. She wasn’t dead. She wasn’t dead. Police station, he thought. That was the only word he repeated throughout his run. When the dirt trail became concrete, suburban houses stood, but still nobody in blue but the watching sky. When the concrete road turned wider, the sign beside it didn’t read I-95. And it was too late to realize they’d got a telephone at home. David sighed. He merely gripped the knife’s handle harder, so the pain made him forget. Eventually, the highway sloped down. Reduced to an intersection with only three zebra crossings. He walked past the bare concrete without looking both ways. Nobody passed him. People in winter jackets when the trees were still at their warmest color. David lowered his shoulders and pointed his hands around to make himself vulnerable. Waved at cars, too. Everyone passed, and the cars appeared. What if they stayed too close to the sidewalk and got hit by a container truck? “Sir? Sir? Where’s the police station?” he kept asking. Everyone kept pointing at walls and random street corners. He jogged in circles. Then more and more toward more streets and only paused when the wind picked up, irritating his ear when it blew hard on it. Walnut Street, the sign said. Voices drifted down the sidewalk. Not looking at anyone. Just the grout lines of the concrete. “You good. You loyal. You good at slingin’…” “He’s gonna promote you, Qbert. But if you fuck him, you dead…” One of them laughed. It didn’t carry far as a car honked and rendered it silent. “A’ight. I get it. Thank you, man…” David paused while they stopped near a deli. One leaned against the glass, checking his cigarette box. The other stood on the door. “Just make sure yo parents keep cookin’ that Chinese, you feel me?” David stood across the street. The kosher sign. The random callings. Kept talking. About nothing. About names. About places. David took a step forward and crossed the street once again. He put his hands in his pockets, one clutching the handle so hard his sharp nails bent. When he passed by them, their heads subtly following David, a voice— “Hey!” David turned around. One of them smiled. Not at David, but to his side. He looked back, but there was nobody but parked cars. The man’s hand hung open, palm up, already slouching. “Gimme some money, please, sir.” “C’mon, Q, let’s bounce.” The other shifted his weight, smiling. His heel scraped the pavement once again. “Nah, nah.” Q laughed under his breath. “I just need five bucks, yo. You got your wallet?” He trudged forward toward David. Close enough, he could smell the sourness of sweat sticking in his tank top. “Ay, be serious.” The second man glanced down the street, still chuckling. “You can get five bucks off the sidewalk.” Q shrugged. His hand stayed out. “Look, five bucks is five bucks. Ain’t that much, right?” David stayed silent. Knife in his pockets still. Tightened it “Do what’s right,” Q whispered. Silence stretched. A car passed. A mosquito buzzing in David’s ear, not Q’s. “Charity, man. “Exactly,” the second man said. “It’s charity, you feel me?” “You and me,” Q pointed at David, then back at himself, “we’re saving everyone’s time.” The vending machine behind Q was red. Nothing but a blotch of red in the peripheral vision, fading out like it was wipeable. But his arm trembled. His veins pumped and wanted to rupture if it didn’t move in the next millisecond. His bones moved weirdly, and his flesh couldn’t stretch. Joints pinching on his inner viscera. A soreness of sharp needles, this time, even though they felt small, he could feel each of the thousands of them. Fall. Swung his arm. Coca-Cola, red. Orange sky. Foliage rotting and golden. All watched. He continued and didn’t stop, cutting through the air, and the knife’s blade disappeared inside Q’s left-hand chest. David yanked the knife out, and it sliced the second man’s throat. A gust of air, like someone exhaling for a long time. The flaps of skin were pulled out slightly. But the wind swept by. His arms felt dry. Gazed at the asphalt. A layer of skin, deflated. Yet the sun beat on with its light, cars passing without pausing to even look for a second, while shadows flickered around where he stood. The pain in his ankle returned. Perhaps it was when he killed another man that it was gone. He wasn’t that sure. However, something is better than nothing, he thought. How many days had he gone with the pain? That was the question he mouthed while he ran back to the sidewalk. Nobody batted an eye despite the knife’s reflective glare. An old couple walking by. Held hands. David walked up to them. Stopped them in their tracks. Under their thick clothing were only their facelessness and four limbs. He only looked down, with the knife barely in view. Before a voice could say “Good morning,” David put up the knife’s tip over to their dangling fingers. An instinctive sigh from their absent mouths. Confused? Disappointed? He continued, slowly, and jolted his hand up to catch them by surprise. Both combined made a rupture. They deflated and fell slowly to the rough concrete, like disassembling a curtain. Their clothes dropped first. No blood, no guts, no tears. He could take the sweater and the black leather shoes, wear them, and nobody would notice him. “911, what’s your emergency?” Turned back. Waist hurt. The knife accidentally sliced through a short person’s forehead. Backpack on the floor. He twisted his ankle, and it was merely numb. The knife paired well. People turned their backs and stared at him. Most froze. Some ran away and dropped their handbags. The pain mustn’t return, he said. And so he ran across the street and drew the knife on as many people as possible. After the third or sixth person, he lost count. A hooded figure. A small, anthropomorphic blotch of color. David dragged the knife across the air and, with a touch, the long-sleeved clothing became the entirety of a person. The wind didn’t fight back. When the clothes had fallen, it blew in the same direction as David’s swing. “You’re under arrest,” echoed, as if he were in a skybox. But the count continued. His ankle was still painless, and it was cathartic. And he blinked. “You have the right to remain silent…” POLICE in large text. An officer in front of him was wearing a thick vest. His hands gripped the upper part of the torso plate. Behind him, someone in a suit. Perhaps a lawyer? A bank manager? David was only stopped when that person almost became a victim. “…You have the right to an attorney. If you cannot afford an attorney, one will be provided for you at government expense.” By the end, the streets had as many rows of beige-colored spots—clothes and skin as one—as the white lines of crossings.







[[File:Fourteenth image.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


A gold-toned sign. David blinked at its reflections. HON. CATHERINE NGUYEN
PRESIDING. “All rise,” Clerk with the wavy hair said. Judge entered from the back wall, taking her soft-padded seat. The squeak overwhelmed the silence, and David’s ear rang as it did in the parking lot. Flinch. He gazed beside him at his lawyer, staring down at his loafers, with eyebrows eager to cover his eyes and ears. “Please be seated,” Judge stated. Everyone, the jury, had sat down except Clerk and Foreperson. Clerk adjusted his collar. “The jury has returned its penalty verdict.” “In the case of Commonwealth v. David S. Rosenfeld,” he said, “we, the jury, find the following: One or more statutory aggravating circumstances have been proven beyond a reasonable doubt. We further find mitigating circumstances, but the aggravating circumstances outweigh the mitigating circumstances. It is the unanimous verdict of this jury that the sentence shall be death.” David followed the lawyer because his black suit and tie were identical to his. They both took a deep breath. But David was breathing manually, and stared at the floor, the darkness, while knowing he could admire the golden sign again. “Verdict of the jury,” Clerk declared as Foreperson handed him a piece of paper. It bore as much weight as the nineteen years David carried, but it slipped and fell, needing a few more seconds to fold it so she could grab it more easily. Clerk then passed it to Judge without a problem. “The Court will review the verdict for compliance with Section 9711.” Judge merely glanced at it and set it down on the desk. Wind from the air conditioners picked it up, though only slightly. Yet, it took more time to slide before stopping than she did to read it. “The verdict slip is in proper form.” Clerk bit his lips. “The verdict is so entered.” Prosecutor with a blue tie. He rose slowly. “Your Honor, the Commonwealth requests that the jury be polled.” “Very well,” Judge said. Despite Judge’s words, Clerk stood up again. “Ladies and gentlemen of the jury,” he said before his eyes even gazed at them, “I will ask each of you individually: was the verdict just read as to the sentencing determination your true and individual verdict? Juror Number One?” “Yes,” One said. “Juror Number Two?” “Yes,” Two said. The answers continued by the number, stopping at twelve. Their voices covered the crying and scratching and pencil whispers behind David. He imagined the artists sketching Yes’s with squiggly bubble speeches like a comic, while the rest of the people around them, the families—performers—were shadowy figures. When it was finished, Clerk nodded—only once—to determine the validity of all twelve decisions. “The jury has been polled. The verdict as entered is affirmed.” Judge folded her hands. A fold formed behind her back on the padding. Her head turned toward David, making the same stiff movement as Mother, but her collar and long hair made it less distinguishable despite the flat, bright light. “Mr. Rosenfeld,” she stated. “You may address the Court prior to the formal imposition of sentence. You are not required to do so. If you wish to speak, you may proceed.” And yet, the sounds continued. Even the fluorescent lights interrupted the courtroom, buzzing to signal itself as a witness to the scene. The same noises of the parking lot. The same glaring lights where the mural appeared. Flower returned and merely saw his prey, but could never slingshot its black, gooey arms to shoot him in the head. No guts. David stared right into her downtrodden eyes, looking at her fingers as she fiddled with a hangnail. Alas, she merely adjusted her signage. David’s eyes faltered. Looked down, though upward now, with his hands still. Whatever thoughts passed through him stayed there. Whatever plague Flower tried to insert into his mind had deflected, and its signal landed on everyone but him. Judge nodded. Still didn’t glance at him. “Thank you,” she said. “Let the record reflect that the defendant has declined to make a statement.” Lawyer rose. “Your Honor, we respectfully renew all objections to the penalty verdict as being contrary to the weight of the evidence, and we preserve these objections for appeal.” “They are noted and overruled,” Judge said. “The objections are preserved for the appellate record.” Stenographer glanced up. Clerk stood again. “All rise.” Everyone rose. Judge didn’t move. But it excluded the ones in the back. They kept drawing and crying. They weren’t human. The small fences and gate, just behind where David sat, separated. Was it far or near? He couldn’t tell. He could turn back, but his mind would shake, and he’d utter a word too late. David kept his sight at Judge. She picked up a piece of paper in front of her. No exhales. Just mouth breathing. “In accordance with the verdict of the jury and pursuant to Section 9711 of the Pennsylvania Crimes Code, it is the sentence of this Court that the defendant be committed to the custody of the Pennsylvania Department of Corrections, and that the sentence of death be carried out by lethal injection as provided by law.” A pause mere milliseconds long. Judge probably waited for the stenograph to catch up. Beyond that, David thought of nothing. The months of staring at the same green-colored mold in the left corner of the wall numbed him, alongside the sling on his ankle that was about to fall off. “The sentence is entered of record,” Judge continued without looking at Stenographer. “The clerk shall transmit this record for automatic appellate review as required by law.” Only then did she glance toward the jury box. “Members of the jury, thank you for your service in this difficult matter. You are now discharged and excused from further service in this case.” The jurors stood, turned, and left. The door closed behind them. And it was another day to cross the calendar with routine. They all did. Through the small window on the door, they paused. Some talked with solemn faces, as their wrinkles droop down far beyond a frown. Two talked loudly about Islamic terrorism, no longer “domestic.” Most left without saying a word—wiped their glasses at best.







[[File:Fifteenth image.jpg|link=|center|505px]]


David dragged the mattress down to the floor, edges against the bars, and uppercutted the only pillow in his cell. It had a folded mark and a worn texture around the bend. A good exercise. A more innocent Tyson, he thought, and far more tired. But by the fifteenth punch, his muscles had already ached, his sweat had grown too heavy, and he landed on the mattress. Felt hard. Unlike the rusty, metal frame, the concrete floor and the bars couldn’t bend. He glanced toward each metal pole. All but one was vertical. On the horizontal bar, an ant trail formed where a dead fly lay. A puddle of insect juice. That sweet, sweet thing. And there was a man’s figure behind the blur. Homeless-looking. Had a beard thrice his face’s age, for there were white strands of hair hiding among the gray ones. “Howdy.” David looked up from the bunk. The fly disappeared, smaller than he could see. “Hey.” The man jerked his head toward the pillow David sat on. “How much you servin’?” he said as David gazed down at the folded mark. David shrugged. “How ’bout you?” “I got death. I’ll assume you too.” “My lawyer is good,” David said as he huffed a laugh. “He gave me life.” Shooting him a sideways look, the man felt his beard. “C’mon, man. Don’t lie to me.” He tapped his temple. “Let me guess. You went around the streets and started killing people, no?” David said. The man raised his eyebrows and clasped his hands. “‘People,’ huh? Whoa now, PETA. Don’t tell me you’re one o’ them PC types.” “I’m Jewish, all right? Not having a Torah scroll under this bed doesn’t mean I’m not.” “Y’know them kosher trays are fresher. You’ll love prison.” “What’s your name?” David stared at him for a moment. “No. Nothin’. They just say I’m from Oklahoma City.” The Oklahoman scratched at the back of his neck David narrowed his eyes. “You sound like you do.” “I ain’t from here, man. I used to got a number for my name. Them were good times.” “Hey,” David leaned in and stage-whispered, mockery thick in his voice, his hand half-covering his mouth. “You’re white, this isn’t the 1850s, and Abraham Lincoln is in a bill—" The Oklahoman interrupted David with a soft, disappointing inhale. His head was down, and his hand was cupped over, as if trying to silence himself. “I was in the wrong place at the wrong time, man.” He rubbed at the back of his neck. “You know this place, ain’t you?” David sat up a little straighter. “Philly? I’ve lived in its countryside for nineteen years. Never left. What’re you doing coming from Oklahoma?” “Never said I was from there.” “Right,” David said more lowly. “Why’d they say you’re from there, then?” He shrugged. His eyes moved a millimeter toward the bars instead of David. No blur to interrupt David’s interpretation. “I dunno,” the Oklahoman said, syllables merging with one another. David leaned forward. “So, what’s your name?” “Entity… Esmeralda.” “A girl’s name? What, nobody cared to...” “Hm?” Esmeralda the Oklahoman blinked like he missed half of it. “Never mind, forget it.” Esmeralda waved his hand lazily. “No, no. I was dozin’ off. I ain’t Latino if you’re still wonderin’.” “What’s your family history, then? Or how about yourself?” David asked, pursing his lips. Scratching at his temple again, Esmeralda paused to think. “Well,” he finally came up with one. “Fun fact. I used to have a sword. It was completely black.” David tilted his head, interested despite the majestic, pristine pillow in his peripheral vision. “Medieval? Japanese?” “Just black.” Esmeralda pointed with both hands, making a finger gun but with only the barrel, tracing an arc in the air with his index fingers. “Got a bent handle. It’s got these wires that wrap around its two handles.” David frowned and nodded. “Black? Hm. Flint. What’d you use them for? Circumcision?” Esmeralda barked out a laugh. “Good one. Nah, wasn’t it.” His eyes softened for a moment. The bare light bulb above lost its reflections. “I also got a cape like you guys’ Superman. It was red, but it’s got a bit o’ silver.” “You flew with that?” David asked, smiling now. The lights flickered. “I did.” Esmeralda nodded. “I used to have ’em both, and I’d sweep an army. I enjoyed it, I was right in their wheelhouse, and the good people liked me, but I’d only noticed now.” David betrayed his mattress, shifting himself to lean back against the wall with peeling paint. His hands were behind his head to cushion it. “That sounds fun,” he said. “But it does get disappointing when you get called by your Ma to tidy up the tiny, green soldiers.” A nod. “Of course, of course.” For a moment, they sat there with the lights overhead. When it flickered once, it stayed silent. The serenity went by for minutes. At one point, a buzzing ran across the room. Fluorescent lights emerging from above. David ended the cycle. “Is this what we do for… the rest of our lives now?” he drawled. “Dream and speak it out loud?” Esmeralda let out a breath through his nose, almost a chuckle if his throat was slightly weaker. “I guess so. And this ain’t no Green Mile either. Think of the injection like a Christmas present from Tom Ridge. Get it?” Alas, a laugh. “That’s funny,” David said, grinning, while he shifted backward and forward like he was trained to do it while wearing a collar. “It is,” Esmeralda said, amused with himself. “It surely is.” From the hallway, a guard banged on a door somewhere out of sight. “Quiet down, ladies!” The lights stopped flickering then. David lowered his voice. “Well, it’s dinner time now.” Esmeralda sat up again. He held the bars with both his hands, gripping them until his hairy, callused fingers turned yellow and numb. “We got no more time to talk?” “Yeah,” David said as he got up. No more pain in his ankle. “I’ve gotta eat this kosher meal you were talking about. There’s a newspaper here, too.” He held up the tray like proof. “Which prison you goin’?” David stared at the door. “I don’t know.” “Well. Good to know you, David. In another life.” A moment to freeze, but not for the cold. “In another life,” David stated. A muscular guard, veins popping out of the skin, striding over to David’s cell, toward him like a march. He rattled a key chain that was no longer a perfect circle. The keys looked like a solid block of shiny metal, save for a couple of shabby, gold strips. The two men exchanged nothing but a stare that lasted a second longer than the guard wanted it to be. And when the guard stepped to the side, he blocked David’s view of Esmeralda. A nametag on his chest. SMITH. Smith opened the food slot, sliding over a red plastic tray. Inside, wrapped in newspaper, lay a hamburger soaked in a slimy sauce and a piece of bread. David looked up after losing his appetite, fast, as he needed to scold Esmeralda for his lies. But he’d gone. It was merely his bed, pillow, toilet, and metal protrusions out of the wall and floor as excuses for a table and chair. “I’m eating now, all right? You’ll live to see another day,” he mouthed to himself. After finishing the meal without dry-heaving a few times, he threw up a few cartilage pieces. It bathed in the clear, brownish slime his protein had been drowned in. He needed something to clear his mind. The newspaper. David tore it and read the part that remained dry. New Guinea declared a Protectorate by Britain. 1884. Nothing younger than that, as the most recent news was a few decades earlier. He flipped the torn, little piece of paper. SYNOPSIS, it read as the title. David coughed, grabbed the tray and the paper, and placed them on the table. He sat down on the lidless toilet. Cold and harsh. But cold was always clean, and human warmth suggested something dirtier than the rust on his metal bed could ever be. Humid was the word, he thought, but David glanced off, and his eyes never returned to his surroundings. Instead, he raised the paper right up. It was the 1870s. David merely remembered it as the years of the Civil War. He wished Abraham Lincoln could read this with him, he thought. A punchy premise: A British naturalist traveling to Papua becomes separated from his guides. The odyssey continued as the Brit was taken in by a remote river community feared by Europeans for ritual violence. He was mistaken for a spirit or sorcerer, however, as he survived a trip from a local, powerful drug, and a chunk of his flesh was ripped apart by a snake, somehow. The villagers interpreted it as an ancestral sanction, and he was allowed to live among them. Months later, he was cheered on by certain groups of the tribe. The Brit responded in confusion, but followed them regardless. They were excited because the Brit hadn’t participated in their tribe’s legendary, biannual ritual. A mortuary ceremony meant to return a body to the gods in the clouds. He was appointed by the chief to choose among the healthy-looking in a quarantined hut. The Brit chose the most muscular body. He carried it onto the bonfire and placed its back on sharp pieces of wood that stuck out and stabbed through the skin. While carrying out his role, however, he felt his arms twitch. Could it be his faulty twigs? But the most tattooed men emerged behind him, carrying machetes. They pointed the curved, blood-rusted blades at him and bellowed. The Brit turned behind him for a split second. Black layers of the bodybuilder’s skin peeled off to reveal lighter ones. Smelled of burnt pork. He ran into the darkness and, guided by the stars and snakes, managed to lose the tribesmen. He survived for months before being rescued by a trading vessel, later publishing an account of the tribe and its rituals, though omitting his part in the ceremony. Later becoming an author, the Brit received wild acclaim following a measured notice from Alfred Russel Wallace, and was subsequently reviewed in The Athenaeum. Decades later, the Brit returned to the region as an advisor to a colonial expedition. As he loitered around a nearby beach, his ship glowing in moonlight, he looked up at the stars once again, and the ocean’s perfect, brown water mirrored their reflections. The stars cascaded. And then a slight rustle behind him. He turned back. A throat-burning scream across the coast. “For my father!” The machete ran through the Brit’s head, and it thud on the sand like throwing away a disappointing treasure.
